hook: You already know that triangles are everywhere — in bridges, architecture, and art — and you've worked with their basic properties before. But how can engineers be certain that two triangles built in different places are truly identical? In this unit, you'll discover the surprisingly elegant rules that let us prove two triangles are exactly the same, no measuring tape required.
concept: The unifying idea is that triangles are rigid, predictable shapes — knowing just the right combination of sides and angles guarantees two triangles are identical, a property called 'congruence via minimal conditions.'

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Triangle Angle Sum and Exterior Angle Theorems
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to use the fact that a triangle's angles always sum to $180°$ — and that an exterior angle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles — to find missing angle measures.
Master how to classify any triangle by its side lengths (scalene, isosceles, equilateral) and by its angle measures (acute, right, obtuse) so you can quickly recognize a triangle's key properties.

You will learn to identify and name corresponding sides and angles between two congruent triangles, understanding that $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle DEF$ carries precise meaning about which parts match.
Apply the Side-Side-Side and Side-Angle-Side shortcuts to determine whether two triangles must be congruent using only three carefully chosen measurements.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Triangle Congruence: ASA, AAS, and HL
topic_roadmap.description: Expand your congruence toolkit by using Angle-Side-Angle, Angle-Angle-Side, and the Hypotenuse-Leg theorem to prove triangles congruent in even more situations, including right triangles.
You will learn to write logical, step-by-step geometric proofs that use triangle congruence to conclude that specific sides or angles — marked CPCTC — must also be equal.
Discover the special angle and side relationships that always appear in isosceles and equilateral triangles, and apply the Base Angles Theorem to solve problems and write proofs.
